coredata.dc:title: PC12 live cell ultrasensitive neurotransmitter signaling using high quantum yield sulphur doped carbon dots and its extracellular Ca2+ ion dependence
coredata.dc:creator: Gupta, Abhishek
Nandi, Chayan Kanti
coredata.dc:description: Synthesizing bright, photostable and non-toxic doped fluorescent material is always a great challenge for its successful application. On the other hand, because of its very low concentration down to nM level, detection of neurotransmitter inside live cell is also very challenging. In this report, a novel one-step synthetic method for the synthesis of highly bright multicolor fluorescent sulphur doped carbon dots (CNDs), a new class of carbogenic nanomaterials, with quantum yield (QY) upto 58% is reported. The synthesized CNDs show ultrasensitive sensing for neurotransmitter dopamine (DA) detection without any surface functionalization using fluorometric assay down to 47pM in water and 92pM in blood plasma. Interestingly, the CNDs show very high selectivity to DA even in presence of high concentrations of other relevant interfering molecules. The CNDs are very efficient to detect the DA in live PC12 cell under hypoxic environment. We further provide fruitful information on Ca2+ dependent DA release inside neuron cells. It is still a debatable problem whether extracellular Ca2+ accelerates the DA release from synaptic vesicles or stored intracellular Ca2+ is sufficient for DA release. Our data shows that indeed extracellular Ca2+ influx inside neuron cells accelerates the DA release.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
High quantum yield
Neurotransmitter dopamine
Live cell sensing
Extracellular Ca2+ influx

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdateend pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure e-component body mmlmath acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast highlightsabst primabst ref specialabst vitae

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Steady state and time resolved fluorescence spectroscopy
Confocal imaging of live cells (Human cell lines)
Binding and fluorescence quenching mechanism of DA with CNDs
Detection method for DA in Live PC-12 cell lines

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterization of the synthesized CNDs (a) AFM image (height profile shows the average size is ∼5nm (b) TEM image (inset shows the particle size distribution) (c) Raman spectrum (inset showing the XRD pattern) and (d) XPS spectrum shows three major peaks of C1s, O1s and S2p, respectively.

(a) FTIR spectra of CNDs, dopamine and DA-CNDs conjugate (b) Zeta Potential of synthesized CNDs before and after binding with dopamine (inset shows the UV lamp images of CNDs without and with addition of dopamine) (c) Titrimetric fluorescence quenching of CNDs with increasing concentration of DA (inset is the Stern-Volmer fitting to calculate the DL) and (d) plasma proteins spiked with increasing concentration of dopamine, resulted continuous quenching of CNDs (inset is the Stern-Volmer fitting to calculate the DL).
(a) and (b) are the relative fluorescence intensities change of CNDs in the presence of various other interfering biomolecules (50μM) individually added to the CNDs.

(a) Confocal images of PC-12 cells incubated with CNDs, (i) bright field image, confocal images taken by excited at (ii) 401, (iii) 488 and (iv) 639nm lasers and confocal images of PC12 cells that are incubated with CNDs in response to hypoxic stimulation. The cells were incubated with CNDs for 30min in (b) 50% N2 reduced hypoxic buffer, Scale bar=50μm.

(a) Time dependent confocal image of the live PC12 neuron cells in hypoxic condition Scale bar=50μm (b) intensity bar graph of Fig. 5a, shows a continues decrease of fluorescence intensity and (c) confocal image of the PC 12 cells in hypoxic condition with the addition of 500μM Ca2+ ions and 2min of incubation period.
(a) Confocal imaging of the PC-12 cell in hypoxic condition with the increase of Ca2+ ion concentration. Data were measured within 2min of incubation time. Scale bar showing the size of 50μm.

head.ce:title: PC12 live cell ultrasensitive neurotransmitter signaling using high quantum yield sulphur doped carbon dots and its extracellular Ca2+ ion dependence

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Basic Sciences, Indian Institute of Technology Mandi, Himachal Pradesh, 175001, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Synthesizing bright, photostable and non-toxic doped fluorescent material is always a great challenge for its successful application. On the other hand, because of its very low concentration down to nM level, detection of neurotransmitter inside live cell is also very challenging. In this report, a novel one-step synthetic method for the synthesis of highly bright multicolor fluorescent sulphur doped carbon dots (CNDs), a new class of carbogenic nanomaterials, with quantum yield (QY) upto 58% is reported. The synthesized CNDs show ultrasensitive sensing for neurotransmitter dopamine (DA) detection without any surface functionalization using fluorometric assay down to 47pM in water and 92pM in blood plasma. Interestingly, the CNDs show very high selectivity to DA even in presence of high concentrations of other relevant interfering molecules. The CNDs are very efficient to detect the DA in live PC12 cell under hypoxic environment. We further provide fruitful information on Ca2+ dependent DA release inside neuron cells. It is still a debatable problem whether extracellular Ca2+ accelerates the DA release from synaptic vesicles or stored intracellular Ca2+ is sufficient for DA release. Our data shows that indeed extracellular Ca2+ influx inside neuron cells accelerates the DA release.

ce:section.ce:para: Dopamine (DA), the most important neurotransmitter in the central nervous system plays crucial role in many biological functions. The abnormal level of DA is the main cause of many death alerting neuro-disorder diseases such as Parkinson, Schizophrenia and Huntington [1,2]. The brain sends out signals through DA to increase the breathing rate in a less oxygenated condition. DA also plays important role in other organs of human body [3]. For example in blood vessels, DA inhibits norepinephrine release and acts as a vasodilator. Abnormal DA level in either urine or blood indicates pheochromocytomas and paragangliomas, which are rare tumors arising in neural crest tissue. As a result monitoring DA is one of the major concerns in neuroscience. However, the concentration of DA inside cellular environment is substantially low (0.01–1μM) [4]. In addition, many other biomolecules, especially ascorbic acid, L-DOPA and several amino acids interfere in the detection of DA.

Many methods for the detection of DA including high-performance liquid chromatography, surface enhanced Raman scattering, electrochemical and chemiluminescence assays are developed [5–8]. Although, among the methods, electrochemiluminescent method provided ultrasensitive detection (Table S1), however, these techniques have several limitations such as the requirement of costly instrumental facilities, complex sample preparation, time-consuming procedures and moreover the requirement of trained personnel. Further, in electrochemical sensing simultaneous oxidation of ascorbic acid and other interfering molecules again reproduce DA in the solution, thus could interfere in the detection and provide erroneous results [9]. Reports are also available on the colorimetric detection, but they suffer from the complex surface functionalization and moreover none of the above methods are good for real live cell monitoring of DA (Table S1) [10].

In this report, we present a new rapid fluorescence based method for the ultrasensitive detection of DA in live PC12 neuron cells using as prepared sulphur doped high QY CNDs (58%). Unlike the semiconductor quantum dots or metal nanoparticles, CNDs could easily be synthesized using a single step reaction. Although the real structure of these CNDs are still not known [11,12], however because of easy low cost synthetic methods, high brightness, photo stability, nontoxic and moreover easily cell permeability, CNDs showed great potentials in sensing [13–15], light harvesting, drug delivery, catalytic activity and in bioimaging [16–20]. Further, chemical doping in nanomaterials can tailor its electronic property to increase the QY and chemical reactivity [21,22]. For example, nitrogen doped graphene exhibited high CO tolerance for oxygen reduction reaction [23]. Lu et al., developed a method to use catalytic property of −OH rich CNDs for reducing and stabilizing agent for the preparation of noble metal nanoparticles for the detection of glucose and thiocyanate [24,25]. Several reports (Table S2) show the extensive increase in QY of CNDs when doped either by nitrogen or co-doped with sulphur. Unfortunately, high QY CNDs doped only with sulphur was rarely been reported (67%) [26]. Although, recently few CNDs are reported for the detection of DA however, none of them showed ultrasensitive and selective DA detection as presented in the current study. Further, they have been used only in electrochemical detection but not in real live cell with such a high sensitivity [27–30]. The detection limit (DL) for DA was found to be as low as 47pM in water and 92nM in blood plasma, respectively. The present CNDs were also very efficient to detect the DA in live PC12 cell.

We further shed light on a long term debate on Ca2+ dependent DA release from synaptic vesicles [31]. It is well known that the release of DA from synaptic vesicles requires certain amount of Ca2+, which forms complex with the vesicles after receiving the impulse from presynaptic neuron cells [32]. Once sufficient amount of Ca2+ is complexed with the vesicles the DA starts releasing from the vesicles. However, whether the intracellular Ca2+ inside the neuron cell is sufficient or certain amount of Ca2+ is influx through Ca2+ gated channel is still a debate. Our data clearly showed that the quantitative extracellular Ca2+ influx inside neuron cells accelerated the dopamine release.

All glasswares were washed with aqua regia (3 HCl: 1 HNO3), followed by rinsing several times with double distilled water. Sodium citrate, sodium thio sulphate, ascorbic acid, glucose, sucrose, aspartic acid, lysine, alanine, phenyl alanine, tyrosine, l-DOPA, KCl, CaCl2, blood plasma, tyramine, methylthiazolyldiphenyl-tetrazolium bromide (MTT) and catechol were purchased from Sigma Aldrich, India. Double distilled 18.3mΩ deionized (DI) water (Elga Purelab Ultra, Vivendi Water Systems Ltd.) was used throughout for the preparation of solutions.

The UV–vis absorption spectra were recorded using Shimadzu UV–vis 2450 spectrophotometer Shimadzu Corporation. Japan. The spectra were collected using a quartz cuvette of 10mm path length with volume of 1mL. All the measurements were repeated at least three times.

FTIR spectra of CNDs and CNDs/DA conjugate were measured using a Agilent FTIR spectrophotometer, (Model No. K8002AA carry 660, USA) equipped with a horizontal attenuated total reflectance (ATR) accessory that contains a zinc selenide crystal and operates at 4cm−1 resolution. Sample preparation done by putting the dry CNDs powder on ATR plate followed by scanning from 400 to 4000cm−1.

Zeta potential measurements were carried out on a Malvern Zetasizer Nano system equipped with 633nm He-Ne laser. Water soluble CNDs have been used for the measurement. The dynamic viscosity of water (solvent) is 8.90×10−3 dyn.s/cm2 and refractive index is 1.33 at 25°C. 1mL of particle suspension (concentration=1mg/mL) was used in polystyrene cuvette (Malvern-DTS1061, United Kingdom). All the measurements were carried out at pH=7.4. The average zeta potential was measured for 10 runs in 10s time interval. The average of at least five measurements was reported in each data.

AFM analysis of the synthesized CNDs for particle size determination was carried out using a Bruker AFM Instruments (Model – Dimension Icon). Standard Veeco tapping mode silicon probes were used for scanning the samples. Typically, aqueous suspensions of CNDs samples were dried on silicon substrate for 3h. Once dried, samples were placed on the AFM stage and scanned.

X-ray photo-electron spectroscopy (XPS) with auger electron spectroscopy (AES) (Model No. PHI 5000 Versa Prob II, Chanhassen, MN) FEI Inc. and C60 sputter gun have been used for characterization and scanning the spectra from C1s, N1s, O1s and S2p region. Al Kα X-ray radiation was used as the source for excitation (1486.8eV, 500mm). Samples were loaded on copper strips, and surface adherence done by double sided adhesive tape.

Dynamic Light Scattering (DLS) measurements were performed using a Malvern Zetasizer Nano Series (Malvern Instruments, Westborough, MA, USA) equipped with a He-Ne laser (λ=633nm, max 5mW) and operated at a single scattering angle of 173°. The dynamic viscosity of water (solvent) is 8.90×10−3 dyn.s/cm2 and refractive index is 1.33 at 25°C. 1mL of particle suspension was used (concentration=1mg/mL) in a polystyrene cuvette (maintained pH=7.4).

The particle size and dispersity of the synthesized CNDs were performed using a TECNAI G2 200kV TEM (FEI, Electron Optics) electron microscope with 200kV input voltage. TEM grids were prepared by placing 5μL diluted and well sonicated sample solution on a carbon coated copper grid and evaporated the solution at room temperature completely.

XRD diffraction patterns were recorded on Rigaku Smart Lab X-Ray diffractometer using Cu Kα radiation as X-ray source (λ=1.5418Å) at room temperature. The voltage and current for the measurement were kept 45kV and 100mA respectively. 1mL of dialysed CNDs was dried inside a 37°C incubator to get powder sample for analysis.

The Raman spectrum was measured by the confocal Microscope Raman spectrometer (Horiba Scientific, XploRA ONE) equipped with 633nm laser. Sample preparation for analysis was done by putting the drop of CNDs on the quartz slide followed by drying inside a 37°C incubator.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Steady state and time resolved fluorescence spectroscopy
ce:section.ce:para: Steady state fluorescence was measured using Horiba Fluorolog-3 Spectrofluorometer. All the experiments including DA detection were performed at room temperature after equilibrating the samples for 15min. The kinetics of the detection was investigated by recording the fluorescence spectra of CNDs, immediately after the addition of dopamine up to 5min (as most of the quenching processes were over within 5min of the contact time). The fluorescence lifetime was measured using Horiba scientific Delta Flex TCSPC system with the desired Pulsed LED Sources. Ludox has been used as an IRF for de-convolution of the spectral value. The photon decays in different channels were fitted with a chi-squared value <1.2. Approximately 3mL of CNDs solution was used for all measurements.

PC-12 and HeLa cells were obtained from NCCS Pune, India. Cells were grown in normal DMEM culture medium with 10% fetal bovine serum (FBS) and 1% penicillin/streptomycin. During staining of cell lines, Lab-Tek® Chamber slide was used, where cell lines medium (with diffused cells) mixed with the same amount of synthesized CNDs in CO2 incubator (Temperature 25°C and 5% CO2) and incubated for 24h. Adhesive cell containing slide has been used after three washing with 1 X PBS solution for removal of extra CNDs and cell culture medium.

The MTT cell proliferation assay measures the cell proliferation rate and conversely, when metabolic events lead to apoptosis or necrosis, the reduction in cell viability. The in-vitro cytotoxicity was measured using a standard MTT assay. HeLa cell were seeded into 96-well cell culture plate at 104/well and then incubated for 24h at 37°C with 5% CO2. The cells were incubated with varying concentration of CNDs for 72h, followed by 1h incubation with the MTT reagent. The optical density of the sample was recorded using multimode plate reader (TECAN).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Confocal imaging of live cells (Human cell lines)
ce:section.ce:para: Imaging of HeLa and PC 12 cells were performed using Nikon Eclipse TI-U system, equipped with three lasers (401, 488 and 639nm) and the corresponding filters. The images were analyzed using software NIS Element AR. 60x Nikon oil immersion objective having 1.4 NA was used. For confocal imaging the cells were incubated with CNDs as desired. Hypoxic condition in cell was produced by inserting different volume of N2 gas.

CNDs were synthesized using a simple microwave assisted pyrolysis method. 25mL trisodium citrate solution (0.2M) and 25mL of sodium thiosulfate (0.6M) were added into a 50mL beaker. The mixture was heated inside a domestic microwave at 600W for 6min. The resulting material was dissolved in 50mL of distilled water. The solution was filtered by Whatman filter paper. 1.5mL of the filtered solution was centrifuged twice (Sorvall Lynx 6000, thermo scientific) at 23000rpm for 30min each. The resulting pellets were removed each time and the final supernatant was dialyzed with 3–5kD dialysis membrane against the deionized water for 48h. The dialyzed CNDs were used for experiments.

All the quenching experiments for DA detection were performed at room temperature. The fluorescence was measured in 1mL quartz cuvette. To calculate the DL, a series of concentration ranging from 0 to 2×104 nM of DA were added to a fixed concentration of CNDs. The final volume was kept as 1mL. The fluorescence emission spectra were recorded after incubation of the mixture for 15min. Finally, the DL was measured by using the well-known Stern-Volmer plot as mentioned later in the result and discussion section. The detection of DA in plasma was measured following the same protocol, however in presence of 25% plasma solution in PBS (0.2M, pH 7.0) buffer. Each measurement was repeated for three times.

The selectivity of DA detection was examined in presence of other relevant interfering molecules that are mainly present in the cytoplasm (ascorbic acid, glucose, sucrose, aspartic acid, lysine, alanine, tyrosine, l-DOPA, K+ ions, Ca2+ ions, tyramine and phenyl alanine). Here 50μM of each above mentioned molecule was incubated with a fixed concentration of CNDs for 15min and the fluorescence was measured at room temperature in PBS (0.2M, pH 7.4) buffer solution. The selectivity was further tested in presence of a mixture of all these molecules with and without addition of DA. Each measurement was repeated for three times.

The average size of the CNDs was found to be ∼5nm as confirmed by the height profile in the AFM image (Fig. 1 a). The homogenous size distribution of CNDs was confirmed by TEM images (Fig. 1b). The DLS result (Fig. S1) further supports the size of the CNDs. The two closely peaks in Raman spectrum at 1580cm−1 (G band) and 1335cm−1 (D band) (Fig. 1c), represent the E2g mode of graphite and the disordered structure of graphene, respectively The XRD pattern (inset of Fig. 1c) showed a broad diffraction peak at 24.7 θ. XPS (Figs. 1d and S2) data confirm the presence of OH, COOH, CO, CSC and SOX 2− surface functional groups in CNDs.

The three peaks at 166.0, 282.9 and 530.8eV in XPS spectrum can be attributed to S2p, C1s and O1s, respectively suggesting that the synthesized CNDs contained C, O and S with% atom ratio of C: O: S=74.6: 24.0: 1.4. The peaks in the high resolution XPS of C1s spectrum at 283.1, 284.3, 285.0 and 287.0eV are assigned to CC and CO/CO, respectively (Fig. S2a). The three peaks at 530.0, 531.0 and 534.2eV in O1s spectrum are assigned to OH and CO/COOH groups, while the four peaks at 159.8, 161.4, 166.3 and 167.8 in S2p spectrum are assigned to the C−S−C and −SOx 2− groups, respectively (Fig. S2b, c). The presence of various functional groups in the surface of CNDs was further confirmed by FTIR. The peaks at 1646, 1589 and 1373cm−1 in the FTIR spectrum (Fig. 2 a) suggesting the presence of CO, CO and COOH stretching vibrations. Peak at 665cm−1 for CS/CSC bending and the broad peak at 3383cm−1 is attributed to OH stretching vibrations. CNDs are readily water soluble and homogeneously dispersed in the solution.

Although both the crystalline and amorphous nature of the CNDs is reported [33,34], however, till date there is no report on the real chemical structure obtained from single crystal XRD. In the present study, the powder XRD data suggested that the present CNDs is amorphous in nature along with graphitic like core structure as obtained from the Raman spectrum [35]. The FTIR and XPS data, further, confirmed the presence of the surface functional groups like OH, COOH, CSC, CO and SOx 2−.

A general trend in normal molecular fluorescence is to follow the Kasha−Vavilov’s rule, where excitation independent emission for the ground vibrational level of the excited state is observed [36]. However, the as synthesized CNDs shows the excitation dependent multicolor fluorescence, where the emission maxima is gradually red shifted to the longer wavelength with decrease in intensity when the excitation wavelength changes from 280nm to 500nm (Fig. S3). The origin of the above excitation-dependent fluorescence CNDs, violating the Kasha−Vavilov’s rule of excitation independent emission is not clear and remains a topic of debate [12]. Our data suggested that the absorbed species are mainly non-emissive in nature as no fluorescence was observed when excited at the absorption maximum (220nm). The maximum fluorescence was observed in a non-absorbing excitation position at 280nm. Starting from the quantum confinement [37,38], the recent accepted mechanism is the presence of different surface defects and variable oxidation states that give rise to different chromophoric groups [38–40] Whether the fluorescence originates from a single particle with multichromophoric groups or from different particles, is still debatable [41]. The important role of either individual surface groups and core or their combined effect has also been established [42]. Recently, we have demonstrated the presence of multiple emissive sub-states which cause spectral migration of fluorescence [12]. The excitation dependent behavior was found to be guided by the relative population of these sub-states. Finally, we concluded that the red-shifting phenomena originate from an individual entity with an ensemble of energy substates but not from a quantum confinement due to mixture of particles or due to the presence of multichromophoric groups.

An extensive quenching of the CNDs fluorescence (at 425nm when excited at 310nm) occurred upon addition of 50μM of DA (Fig. S4), indicating the interaction of CNDs with DA. This was further conformed by the UV lamp image of conjugate upon excitation with 365nm UV lamp source (inset of Fig. 2b), and decreased in zeta potential from −24.4mV to −12.2mV (Fig. 2b) and the extensive decrease of the FTIR peak intensity at around 2800–3200cm−1, which is responsible for NH stretching in DA (Fig. 2a). To determine the detection limit, different concentrations of DA in the range from 0 to 2×104 nM were investigated. Fig. 2c showed a gradual decrease in fluorescence intensity with increasing concentration of DA.

To calculate the DL, we follow the well-known Stern–Volmer quenching method, which is described by the following equation.
F 0
F
− 1 =
K sv
C
Where, Ksv is the Stern–Volmer quenching constant, C is the DA concentration, F0 and F are the fluorescence intensities of CNDs at 425nm in the absence and presence of DA.

The DL was found to be as low as 47pM at a signal-to-noise ratio (S/N) of 3 with a fitting parameter of 0.997 (inset of Fig. 2c). This is so far the best report for ultrasensitive detection of DA using any fluoremetric methods and using CNDs (Table S1). The kinetic measurement shows that most of the fluorescence quenched within 3min after addition of DA (Fig. S5). Inspired by the ultra-sensitivity and fast response, we immediately performed the DA sensing in real plasma samples. An immediate extensive fluorescence quenching with a DL of 92pM was obtained (Fig. 2d).

To determine the selectivity, the influence of various relevant interfering molecules present in the cytoplasm (ascorbic acid, glucose, sucrose, aspartic acid, lysine, alanine, tyrosine, L-DOPA, K+ ions, Ca2+ ions, tyramine and phenyl alanine) was checked. It is observed that other molecules had no influence to the DA detection. Even ascorbic acid the most interfering agent, instead any quenching, shows little fluorescence enhancement (Fig. 3 a, b). This is one of the great advantages of the present system, as generally in electrochemical detection ascorbic acid (with almost similar oxidation potentials) extensively interferes in DA detection. We also checked the DA sensing in presence of all these molecular mixture with including and excluding DA (Fig. S6). No change in the fluorescence intensity of the CNDs in the presence of all the molecules (excluding DA) however extensive quenching was observed when DA added to the system.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Binding and fluorescence quenching mechanism of DA with CNDs
ce:section.ce:para: Reports suggested that CNDs with rich of COOH/-OH groups on the surface can bind/passivate DA by the formation of amide (CONH) linkage with the NH2 group of DA [43–45]. However such linkage generally required the reaction activator such as carbodimide or EDC/NHS coupling reagent [44]. On the other hand, DA can also bind to the CNDs via electrostatic interaction or hydrogen bond in between COOH of CNDs and NH2 of DA and it is further strengthened by the aromatic π-π* stacking interaction in between them [46]. Based on the FTIR (Fig. 2a) and selectivity data (Fig. 3 ), the present CNDs and DA complexation could best be fitted to hydrogen bonding along with π-π* stacking interaction. The broad peak at 2800–3300cm−1 in FTIR of DA which is responsible for NH2 stretching vibration was reduced upon complexation with CNDs, probably suggesting the role of −NH2 group in the hydrogen bond formation, as was also reported earlier [47]. Very little fluorescence quenching was observed in CNDs in presence of catechol (structurally similarity with DA but without NH2), which suggests little role of OH groups (Fig. S7).

Several control experiment presented in the selectivity data confirm the role of aromatic ring. For example, no change in the fluorescence signal in presence of nonaromatic amine functional group compounds (like aspartic acid, lysine and alanine) was observed. On the other hand p-tyramine with very closely similar structure to DA showed substantial quenching. Surprisingly, another class of compounds tyrosine, L-DOPA and L-Phenyl alanine, which have both the NH2 group as well as aromatic ring, instead of extensive quenching similar to DA, showed little higher fluorescence quenching than the non-aromatic amine compounds. In this cases a COOH group in the vicinity of NH2 group somehow inhibited the CNDs binding. This data clearly suggested the both the presence of NH2 group and aromatic ring played significant role. The fluorescence quenching of CNDs occurred via the excited electron transfer process as confirmed by the UV–vis and fluorescence lifetime data. No change was observed in the UV–vis absorption spectrum. However, decreased in excited state lifetime after the DA-CNDs complex formation, confirmed the excited state electron transfer process (Fig. S8). A 64% increase in non-radiative decay and 36% decrease in radiative decay suggested that the complex formation increases the non-radiative decay rate.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection method for DA in Live PC-12 cell lines
ce:section.ce:para: The cell viability of the CNDs was measured in HeLa cells using MTT (methylthiazolyldiphenyl-tetrazolium bromide) assay. The results indicated that high concentration of CNDs (15mg/mL) has no toxic effect (Fig. S9). This observation clearly demonstrated that the CNDs have great potential for live cell neurotransmitter signaling. For PC-12 neuron cell imaging and live cell DA detection, initially N2 mediated less oxygenated non dopaminogenic condition was prepared [4]. This can suppressed the endogenous release of DA and thus preventing the quenching of CNDs. Fig. 4 a shows the confocal images of adherent PC12 cell under such condition when excited using 401, 488 and 639nm laser light sources. Similar to the solution based data, bright blue, green and red color emission with decreasing intensity from blue to red was observed (Fig. S3). The bio-imaging in HeLa cell also showed similar multicolor and bright confocal images (Fig. S10). To detect DA under live cell, the PC-12 cells were incubated with CNDs in 50% N2 hypoxic buffer. This can produce sufficient amount of DA inside the cellular cytoplasm and thus fluorescence quenching was observed (Fig. 4b).

We performed further experiments to shed light on a long term debate on Ca2+ dependent DA release from synaptic vesicles [31]. The DA signaling behavior was performed both in presence and absence of extracellular Ca2+. The data showed that in absence of extracellular Ca2+ the CNDs fluorescence in suspended PC12 cells quenched within 25min of the incubation time (Fig. 5 a, b), whereas it took less than 2min to quench the fluorescence of CNDs when 500μM of Ca2+ was added (Fig. 5c).

This clearly suggested that the influx of extracellular Ca2+ that influx inside the cellular cytoplasm increases the level of Ca2+, which finally accelerates the DA release. We checked the quantitative value of the extracellular Ca2+ concentration for the complete quenching of CNDs fluorescence.

To perform this experiment, PC 12 cells were incubated with a series of increasing concentration of Ca2+. The data (Fig. 6
) shows a gradual decrease in fluorescence intensity from 50μM to 250μM of Ca2+ and the complete quenching of fluorescence happened at 500μM (Fig. 5c).

In conclusion, we have developed a novel approach for the ultrasensitive and highly selective neurotransmitter sensing using CNDs synthesized by simple one step microwave heating. Using fluoremetric method, a detection limit of 47pM was observed for neurotransmitter dopamine. The CNDs are also very efficient to detect dopamine in plasma sample (with detection limit of 92pM) and in live PC12 cells under hypoxic condition. Finally we have shown that, not only stored calcium inside cell but certain amount of the extracellular calcium is important for the release of dopamine from synaptic vesicles.
All authors have declared that no competing interest exists.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors acknowledge the home institute (IIT Mandi) and Department of Biotechnology (BT/PR4067/BRB/10/1128/2012) for running the experiments. The support of Advanced Materials Research Center (AMRC), IIT Mandi is acknowledged.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2017.01.145.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Detection of dopamine dynamics in the brain
Molecular pathways of neurodegeneration in Parkinson’s disease
Detecting subsecond dopamine release with fast-scan cyclic voltammetry in vivo
Conjugated polymer nanoparticles for fluorescence imaging and sensing of neurotransmitter dopamine in living cells and the brains of zebrafish larvae
Analysis of microdialysate monoamines, including noradrenaline, dopamine and serotonin, using capillary ultra-high performance liquid chromatography and electrochemical detection
magnetically assisted surface-enhanced raman scattering selective determination of dopamine in an artificial cerebrospinal fluid and a mouse striatum using fe3o4/ag nanocomposite
Electrochemical determination of dopamine in the presence of ascorbic acid based on the gold nanorods/carbon nanotubes composite film
Flow-injection determination of trace amounts of dopamine by chemiluminescence detection
Tellurium-nanowire-coated glassy carbon electrodes for selective and sensitive detection of dopamine
Silver nanoprobe for sensitive and selective colorimetric detection of dopamine via robust ag–catechol interaction
Time-resolved emission reveals ensemble of emissive states as the origin of multicolor fluorescence in carbon dots'
Nitrogen-doped, thiol-functionalized carbon dots for ultrasensitive Hg (II) detection
Carbon dots for naked eye colorimetric ultrasensitive arsenic and glutathione detection
Paper strip based and live cell ultrasensitive lead sensor using carbon dots synthesized from biological media
Carbon quantum dots for optical bioimaging
Upconversion fluorescent carbon nanodots enriched with nitrogen for light harvesting
Carbon nanoparticles as visible-light photocatalysts for efficient co2 conversion and beyond
Carbon nanodots featuring efficient fret for real-time monitoring of drug delivery and two-photon imaging
Electrochemical synthesis of carbon nanodots directly from alcohols
Nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon dots with strong blue luminescence
Binary and ternary doping of nitrogen, boron, and phosphorus into carbon for enhancing electrochemical oxygen reduction activity
Efficient synthesis of heteroatom (n or s)-doped graphene based on ultrathin graphene oxide-porous silica sheets for oxygen reduction reactions
Hydroxyl-rich c-dots synthesized by a one-pot method and their application in the preparation of noble metal nanoparticles
Detection of thiocyanate through limiting growth of aunps with c-dots acting as reductant
Preparation of highly photoluminescent sulfur-doped carbon dots for fe (III) detection
Highly sensitive, stable, and precise detection of dopamine with carbon dots/tyrosinase hybrid as fluorescent probe
Preparation of N-doped carbon quantum dots for high-sensitive detection of dopamine by electrochemical method
A sensitive and reliable dopamine biosensor was developed based on the au@ carbon dots–chitosan composite film
A high performance electrochemical biosensor based on Cu2O–carbon dots for selective and sensitive determination of dopamine in human serum
An ultrasensitive nanowire-transistor biosensor for detecting dopamine release from living PC12 cells under hypoxic stimulation
Nicotine enhancement of dopamine release by a calcium-dependent increase in the size of the readily releasable pool of synaptic vesicles
Intrinsically fluorescent carbon dots with tunable emission derived from hydrothermal treatment of glucose in the presence of monopotassium phosphate
Water-soluble fluorescent carbon quantum dots and photocatalyst design
water-soluble and phosphorus-containing carbon dots with strong green fluorescence for cell labeling
Characterization of electronic transitions in complex molecules
Quantum-sized carbon dots for bright and colorful photoluminescence
Bandgap-like strong fluorescence in functionalized carbon nanoparticles
Single-particle fluorescence intensity fluctuations of carbon nanodots
Common origin of green luminescence in carbon nanodots and graphene quantum dots
Polyhedral oligomeric silsesquioxane functionalized carbon dots for cell imaging
Highly luminescent carbon nanodots by microwave-assisted pyrolysis
Reduced graphene oxide-carbon dots composite as an enhanced material for electrochemical determination of dopamine
Nanosensor for dopamine and glutathione based on the quenching and recovery of the fluorescence of silica-coated quantum dots

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Abhishek Gupta . Mr. Abhishek Gupta is currently a Ph.D Scholar at School of Basic Sciences, Indian Institute of Technology Mandi HP India. He obtained his B.Sc. from MJP R University Bareilly India and M.Sc. Devi Ahilya University, Indore India. His recent research is focused on carbon nanoparticles (C-dots) for toxic metal and biomolecule sensing system.

Dr. Chayan Kanti Nandi. Chayan Kanti Nandi received his PhD in physical chemistry and laser spectroscopy from the Indian Institute of Technology Kanpur India in 2006. After completing two post-doctoral research works from Goethe University Frankfurt am Main Germany (2006–2009) and Princeton University USA, in 2010 he joined as an assistant Professor in the school of basic sciences, Indian Institute of Technology Mandi, Himachal Pradesh, India. He is the recipient of the prestigious Alexander von Humboldt fellow in 2007. Currently, he is an associate professor with the research interest in the field of material science, especially the use of in nanomaterials for target specific drug delivery, biomolecular nanosensors and protein conformational dynamics.
